title: What If? - Lecture Notes
text:
Welcome
This is a Quarto book project based on the textbook “What If?” by Miguel Hernán and James Robins.
The notes are available in two formats:

title: What If? - Lecture Notes
text:
0.1 About this project
These lecture notes follow the structure and content from What If? by Hernán MA, Robins JM.

title: What If? - Lecture Notes
text:
0.2 Navigation
Use the sidebar to navigate between chapters. Each chapter is available as: - A traditional HTML page (click the chapter name) - RevealJS slides (add -slides.html to the chapter URL)

title: 2  Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
2.1 Overview
This chapter introduces the fundamental concepts of causal inference. The content is presented in a book format optimized for reading and reference.

title: 2  Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
2.2 What is Causal Inference?
Causal inference is the process of drawing conclusions about causal connections based on the conditions of the occurrence of an effect.
In observational studies, unlike randomized experiments, we cannot directly observe the counterfactual outcomes. This fundamental problem of causal inference requires us to make certain assumptions and use appropriate methods.

title: 2  Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
2.3 Fundamental Problem
The fundamental problem of causal inference is that for any given individual, we can observe only one potential outcome - the one corresponding to the actual treatment received. We cannot observe what would have happened under a different treatment.

title: 2  Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
2.4 Notation and Framework
Let’s define our notation:

\(Y\): outcome variable
\(A\): treatment variable

\(Y^{a}\): potential outcome under treatment \(a\)

The potential outcome \(Y^{a}\) represents the outcome that would be observed if, possibly contrary to fact, the individual received treatment level \(a\). This counterfactual framework, formalized by Neyman and Rubin, is central to modern causal inference.

title: 2  Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
2.5 Assumptions
To identify causal effects from observational data, we typically need several key assumptions:

Consistency: The potential outcome \(Y^a\) equals the observed outcome \(Y\) when treatment equals \(a\)
Exchangeability: Treatment assignment is independent of potential outcomes (no unmeasured confounding)
Positivity: Every individual has a non-zero probability of receiving each treatment level

These assumptions, sometimes called “identifiability conditions,” allow us to link the observed data distribution to causal quantities of interest.

title: 2  Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
2.6 Summary
This chapter has introduced the basic framework for causal inference. We’ve defined potential outcomes, discussed the fundamental problem of causal inference, and outlined the key assumptions needed for causal identification. In subsequent chapters, we’ll explore methods for estimating causal effects under various scenarios and assumptions.

title: 3  Chapter 2: Randomized Experiments
text:
3.1 Introduction to Randomized Experiments
Randomized experiments are considered the gold standard for causal inference. In a randomized experiment, treatment assignment is determined by a random mechanism, which ensures that treatment groups are comparable in expectation with respect to all baseline characteristics, both measured and unmeasured.

title: 3  Chapter 2: Randomized Experiments
text:
3.2 Why Randomize?
Randomization provides several key benefits:

Eliminates confounding: By randomly assigning treatment, we break any association between treatment and pre-treatment covariates
Balances groups: Treated and untreated groups are comparable in expectation on all baseline characteristics
Simplifies analysis: The average treatment effect can be estimated by a simple difference in means
Provides validity: Results have a clear causal interpretation without requiring untestable assumptions

title: 3  Chapter 2: Randomized Experiments
text:
3.3 Mathematical Framework
In a randomized experiment with treatment \(A \in \{0,1\}\) and outcome \(Y\), we have:
\[P(A=a|L) = P(A=a)\]
where \(L\) represents all pre-treatment covariates. This means treatment assignment is independent of all baseline characteristics. As a result, the average causal effect can be estimated as:
\[E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}] = E[Y|A=1] - E[Y|A=0]\]
The right-hand side is simply the difference in observed mean outcomes between treatment groups, which can be estimated from the data without bias.

title: 3  Chapter 2: Randomized Experiments
text:
3.4 Types of Randomization
There are several approaches to randomization:
Simple Randomization: Each individual is assigned to treatment or control with a fixed probability (e.g., 50%). This is easy to implement but can result in imbalanced group sizes in small samples.
Stratified Randomization: Randomization is performed separately within strata defined by important covariates. This ensures balance on these key variables and can improve precision.
Block Randomization: Randomization is done in blocks to ensure approximately equal numbers in each treatment group. This is particularly useful in sequential trials.
Cluster Randomization: Groups (clusters) of individuals are randomized together. This is used when individual randomization is not feasible.

title: 3  Chapter 2: Randomized Experiments
text:
3.5 Analysis of Randomized Trials
The analysis of randomized experiments is straightforward due to the exchangeability guaranteed by randomization:

Intention-to-treat (ITT): Compare groups as randomized, regardless of actual treatment received. This preserves randomization and provides a valid causal estimate.
Per-protocol: Compare only those who adhered to their assigned treatment. This can be biased if adherence is related to potential outcomes.
As-treated: Analyze based on treatment actually received. This is essentially an observational analysis and loses the benefits of randomization.

The ITT analysis is generally preferred as it maintains the validity of randomization and provides an unbiased estimate of the causal effect of treatment assignment.

title: 3  Chapter 2: Randomized Experiments
text:
3.6 Limitations
Despite their advantages, randomized experiments have limitations:

Ethical constraints: Not all questions can be studied via randomization
Cost: Randomized trials can be expensive and time-consuming
External validity: Results may not generalize to other populations
Compliance: Non-adherence can complicate analysis
Feasibility: Some interventions cannot be randomized

These limitations explain why observational studies remain important and why methods for causal inference from observational data are necessary.

title: 3  Chapter 2: Randomized Experiments
text:
3.7 Summary
Randomized experiments provide the strongest foundation for causal inference by ensuring exchangeability through random treatment assignment. The causal effect can be estimated by a simple comparison of outcomes between randomized groups. However, practical and ethical constraints limit the applicability of randomization, necessitating methods for causal inference from observational data, which we will explore in subsequent chapters.
